---
title: "Internalizing Environmental Risk: Insurance Design and Firm Behavior in Hazardous Industries"
subtitle: ""
author: "Kaleb K. Javier"
format:
  beamer:
    template: "my-template.tex"
    keep-tex: true
    aspectratio: 169
    pdf-engine: xelatex
theme-bg-color: "0D2D52"
---



## Motivation

\BerkeleyLightMode

**Managing environmental liability in risky industries.**

The workhorse tool is **strict liability** --- in the UST setting via CERCLA, RCRA Subtitle I, and parallel state law: the firm pays for the harm it causes, regardless of fault.

\vfill

**The judgment-proof problem (Shavell 1986, 2004).**
When firms' *effective* exposure to liability is less than the harm they might cause --- through limited assets, corporate-form shielding, or imperfect detection --- strict liability under-deters: too much entry, too little care.

\vfill

**Two financial-responsibility (FR) remedies:**

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{(A) Asset / bonding requirements}
  \item \textbf{(B) Compulsory liability insurance}
\end{itemize}

**Shavell's distinguishing question.** Can the insurer **contract on care- and risk-relevant attributes**?

\begin{itemize}
  \item Yes $\Rightarrow$ (B) dominates (A): the premium itself varies with care, doing the incentive work.
  \item No $\Rightarrow$ (B) can be worse than (A): moral hazard dilutes care.
\end{itemize}

---


## The Setting: USTs and the Texas 1998 Reform

\BerkeleyLightMode

\textbf{Research Questions.}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Q1.} Does moving from flat-fee public insurance to risk-rated private insurance change firm behavior on closure, replacement, and confirmed-release outcomes?
  \item \textbf{Q2.} How much of the first-best welfare gain does the risk-rated regime capture, and what determines the residual gap?
\end{itemize}

\vfill

**Federal Financial Responsibility mandate (1988)** --- every UST operator must carry $\$1$M coverage. The \emph{form} of that coverage varies by state:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{38 states} --- public trust fund; uniform per-tank fee. \textit{The insurance contract does not condition on risk attributes.}
  \item \textbf{Texas, Dec 1998} --- fund closed; operators forced into the \textbf{private risk-rated market}. \textit{Premiums condition on age, wall, piping, leak-detection.}
\end{itemize}

\vfill

\centering\small\textit{Tank attributes are recorded in EPA/state inventories under both regimes; the reform changes whether the insurance contract prices on them.}

---


## Contribution

\BerkeleyLightMode

\textbf{Shavell instrument choice \& financial responsibility (theory).}
\hfill Shavell (1982, 1986, 2004); Yin, Pfaff \& Kunreuther (2011)
\vspace{0.15cm}

\textbf{Empirical financial-assurance --- asset / bonding leg.}
\hfill Boomhower (2019); Ho, Hsu, Cha \& Rivera (2018); Yin, Kunreuther \& White (2011)
\vspace{0.15cm}

\textbf{Insurance pricing in environmental settings.}
\hfill Mulder (2022); Yin, Pfaff \& Kunreuther (2007, 2011)
\vspace{0.15cm}

\textbf{Dynamic structural welfare under environmental regulation.}
\hfill Fowlie, Reguant \& Ryan (2016); Muehlenbachs (2015); Blundell, Gowrisankaran \& Langer (2020)

\vfill

\textbf{This paper.} First firm-level empirical test of the \textbf{pure pricing channel} of compulsory liability insurance --- the \textbf{(B)} leg of the Shavell FR menu. Distinguished from (A)-leg work (Boomhower, Ho et al.) and from priced-insurance settings with confounding information channels (Mulder). Pairs reduced-form evidence on closure, replacement, and confirmed releases with a dynamic structural welfare decomposition that bounds the gap to first-best.

---


## Empirical Strategy

\BerkeleyLightMode

- **Quasi-experiment.** Dec 1998: TX closes its uniform-premium fund; operators forced into the risk-rated private market.

- **Sample.** TX + 17 control states (a subset of the 38 uniform-premium states with usable admin data); $\sim 9.8$M tank-years.

- **Reduced form.** DiD with facility FE and cell$\times$year FE.

- **Structural.** Dynamic discrete choice (maintain / exit / replace) estimated by NPL; recovers $(\kappa, K, \gamma_p, \gamma_r)$.

- **Counterfactuals.** Subsidy, Pigouvian, mandate. Welfare $=$ firm surplus $-$ damage PV $-$ outlay PV.

---


## Main Findings

\BerkeleyLightMode

**Reduced form** *(alive-at-reform sample, $N \approx 9.8$M tank-years)*

- TX $\times$ Post raises annual tank-closure probability by **$+1.58$ pp** on a $2.0\%$ baseline
- $4\times$ larger effect in pre-1989 vintage ($+3.1$ pp) — the high-risk SW-heavy stock responds most
- LUST discovery falls **$-1.27$ pp** on a $1.73\%$ baseline; both background and inspection-triggered drop
- Conditional on closing: $+53\%$ relative shift to the **replacement** margin (12.0\% $\to$ 18.3\%); aggregate exit volume unchanged

**Structural (4-parameter DDC)** *(in units of annual revenue $R$, with $R \approx \$10$k as a calibration anchor)*

- $\hat\kappa \approx 26\,R,\; \hat K \approx 5\,R,\; \hat\gamma_p = -0.29,\; \hat\gamma_r = 0.16$
- All three counterfactuals (Subsidy / Pigouvian / Mandate at age 25+) reduce social welfare at $E = \$17$k (Marcus, 2021); optimal $K$ subsidy $s^* \approx 10\%$, far below 50\%

---


##

\BerkeleyMode

::: {.layout-statement}
\begin{center}
\Huge \textbf{A Dynamic Model of Tank Closure}
\end{center}
:::

---

## The Agent's Decision

\BerkeleyLightMode

**The Choice.** Each period $t$, the facility chooses $d_t \in \{\text{Maintain}, \text{Close}\}$.

**The Trade-off.**

- **Maintain:** Earn net revenue, pay insurance premiums.
- **Close:** Exit and recover scrap value $\kappa$ (liquidity of land).

**Flow Utility (Maintain).**

$$
u(a) = \underbrace{R}_{\text{Net Revenue}} - \underbrace{C_j(a)}_{\text{Insurance Premium}}
$$

*Simplifying assumption (full coverage):* environmental risk enters as an *ex-ante* premium cost, not an *ex-post* shock.

---

## The Dynamic Problem

\BerkeleyLightMode

**Bellman equation.**

$$
V(a) = \max \Bigl\{\, \underbrace{u(a) + \beta\, \mathbb{E}[V(a') \mid a]}_{\text{value of maintaining}}, \;\; \underbrace{\kappa}_{\text{scrap value}}\,\Bigr\}
$$

**Continuation value.** The value of keeping the tank active today to preserve the option of operating tomorrow:

$$
V_{\text{cont}}(a) = u(a) + \beta\, \mathbb{E}[V(a') \mid a]
$$

**Optimal stopping rule.** Close if $V_{\text{cont}}(a) < \kappa$.

---

## The Policy Lever: Insurance Regimes

\BerkeleyLightMode

**1. Uniform Premium Pooling ($F$) --- 38 states.**

- Premium: constant $\bar P$, regardless of age or risk.
- Marginal cost of aging: $\;\partial C / \partial a = 0$. No incentive to close aging tanks.

**2. Risk-Based Pricing ($RB$) --- Texas post-1999.**

- Premium: rises with the leak hazard, $C(a) = \lambda \cdot h(a) \cdot L$.
- Marginal cost of aging: $\;\partial C / \partial a > 0$. Aging tanks become increasingly costly.

---

## What's the First Best?

\BerkeleyLightMode

**Social planner's objective.** Internalize remediation costs ($L$) *and* health externalities ($E$):

$$
u_{\text{SOC}}(a) = R - h(a) \cdot (\underbrace{L}_{\text{Remediation}} + \underbrace{E}_{\text{Health}})
$$

**Why risk-based pricing falls short.**

- Actuarial premiums reflect insurer claims data $\rightarrow$ only remediation costs ($L$)
- Health damages ($E$) generate no third-party claims: contamination is unobservable (Marcus, 2021)
- $\Rightarrow$ Even fair risk-based pricing leaves $E$ externalized

**Implication.** $V_{\text{RB}}(a) > V_{\text{SOC}}(a)$ — RBP induces *some* closures, but not enough.

---

## Three Facts to Keep in Mind

\BerkeleyLightMode

**1. Asset depreciation.** Rising leak risk drives tank value strictly down in age ($V'(a) < 0$). The regime determines whether private incentives align with this social decay.

**2. Inefficient retention (Uniform Premium).** Static costs $\bar P$ imply zero marginal cost of aging. Firms ignore risk and retain assets longest: $a^*_{\text{UP}}$.

**3. Partial correction (Risk-Based).** Rising premiums $h(a)\,L$ imply positive marginal cost of aging. Closure accelerates to $a^*_{\text{RB}}$, but still $> a^*_{\text{SOC}}$ due to unpriced health damages.

\begin{center}
\Large
$a^*_{\text{SOC}} \;<\; a^*_{\text{RB}} \;<\; a^*_{\text{UP}}$
\end{center}

---

## Social Optimum Exit Threshold

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Slide_Fig_2_SOC_Exit.pdf}
\end{center}

---

## Risk-Based vs. Social Optimum

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Slide_Fig_3_SOC_RB_Comparison.pdf}
\end{center}

---

## Uniform Premium vs. Social Optimum

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Slide_Fig_5_SOC_FF_Comparison.pdf}
\end{center}

---

## Deadweight Loss Across Regimes

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Slide_Fig_7_Combined_DWL.pdf}
\end{center}

---


##

\BerkeleyMode

::: {.layout-statement}
\begin{center}
\Huge \textbf{Data \& Descriptive Evidence}
\end{center}
:::

---

## FR Compliance: TX is a Private-Insurance Market

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/FigureA_TX_FR_Coverage_Composition.pdf}
\end{center}

---

## Market Structure: Concentrated and Stable

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Figure_TX_FR_Top5_Dominance_HHI.pdf}
\end{center}

---

## Premium Construction

\BerkeleyLightMode

**Texas** — rebuilt from Mid-Continent Casualty rate filings (SERFF, 2006–2024):

$$P_{ijt} \;=\; \text{Base}_t \;\times\; \text{ILF}_{it} \;\times\; \Bigl(1 \;+\; \sum_{k} \lambda_{k,t}\, X_{ijt,k}\Bigr)$$

- $\text{Base}_t$: per-tank base rate, four filing periods
- $\text{ILF}_{it}$: increased-limit factor; fix coverage at federal-minimum \$1M/\$1M
- $X_{ijt}$: tank age, wall, piping, leak-detection technology
- $\lambda_{k,t}$: additive credits/debits from the filed schedule

**Control states** — state financial-responsibility fund records: a per-tank annual assessment, largely flat across years and tank characteristics.

---

## Premium Levels: Texas vs. Control States

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/04e_Premium_by_Year_Treatment.png}
\end{center}

---

## Risk Prediction: Setup

\BerkeleyLightMode

**Estimand.** Annual first-release hazard in the never-yet-leaked risk set:

$$h(t \mid x) \;=\; P\bigl(\,\text{first confirmed release in year } t \mid \text{no prior release}, \; x\,\bigr)$$

In plain words: given what an underwriter can observe at the start of a policy year, what's the probability of a claim?

**Model.** Penalized logistic regression on the pre-treatment panel:

- $x$: 3-year age bins fully interacted with wall construction; state FE; fuel and portfolio controls
- Lasso variable selection; inverse-frequency weights for the 0.70\% base rate
- Out-of-sample CV (10 folds); post-hoc recalibration to the empirical event rate

**Sample.** 3.93M pre-treatment facility-years; $27{,}455$ confirmed first-release events.

$\hat h(x)$ feeds the next figure and the structural-model hazard $h(a_t, W_i)$.

---

## Single-Walled Tanks Drive the Age--Hazard Gradient

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Figure_CV_CellRisk.png}
\end{center}

```{=latex}
\begin{tikzpicture}[remember picture, overlay]
\node[anchor=south west, inner sep=0pt] at ([xshift=10pt, yshift=10pt]current page.south west) {%
  \navbutton[cell-gof][fill=BerkeleyBlue, font=\tiny, inner sep=2pt]{Goodness of fit $\rightarrow$}%
};
\end{tikzpicture}
```

---

## Premiums Track the Risk We Can Predict

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.1cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.80\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Figure_Premium_vs_Hazard_Raw.png}
\end{center}

```{=latex}
\begin{tikzpicture}[remember picture, overlay]
\node[anchor=south west, inner sep=0pt] at ([xshift=10pt, yshift=10pt]current page.south west) {%
  \navbutton[premium-vs-el][fill=BerkeleyBlue, font=\tiny, inner sep=2pt]{Predicted per-tank EL $\rightarrow$}%
};
\end{tikzpicture}
```

---

## Cleanup Cost Distribution

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Figure_cost_distribution_pooled.png}
\end{center}

---


##

\BerkeleyMode

::: {.layout-statement}
\begin{center}
\Huge \textbf{Reduced Form Evidence}
\end{center}
:::

---

## Sample Descriptive Statistics

\BerkeleyLightMode

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.15cm}
\begin{center}
\scriptsize
\input{../../Output/Tables/T_Desc_Stats_Slide.tex}
\end{center}
```

---

## Estimating Sample and Specification

\BerkeleyLightMode

**Static DiD.**

$$Y_{it} \;=\; \beta\,(\text{Texas}_{f(i)} \times \text{Post}_t) \;+\; \alpha_i \;+\; \delta_{ct} \;+\; \varepsilon_{it}$$

**Event study.**

$$Y_{it} \;=\; \sum_{\tau \neq -1} \beta_\tau \cdot \bigl(\text{Texas}_{f(i)} \times \mathbf{1}[t - 1998 = \tau]\bigr) \;+\; \alpha_i \;+\; \delta_{ct} \;+\; \varepsilon_{it}$$

- $\alpha_i$: facility FE. $\delta_{ct}$: cell$\times$year FE with $c = \{\text{wall}\times\text{fuel}\times\text{capacity}\times\text{install cohort}\}$
- *Identifying variation:* within a single tank-vintage cell, in the same calendar year, $\beta$ is the change in the Texas-vs-control closure-rate gap before vs. after Dec 22, 1998

---

## ATT on Tank Closure: $+1.58$ pp on a $2.0\%$ Baseline

\BerkeleyLightMode

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.4cm}
\begin{center}
\input{../../Output/Tables/T_DiD_Stepped_Full.tex}
\end{center}
```

\vfill

\centering
\textit{Cols add facility FE, then cell$\times$year FE. Col (3) is the headline.}

---

## Dynamic Closure Response in Texas

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Fig_ES_Full.pdf}
\end{center}

---

## Heterogeneity: Pre-89 Vintage Drives the Effect

\BerkeleyLightMode

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.15cm}
\begin{center}
\scriptsize
\input{../../Output/Tables/T_HTE_DiD_Vintage.tex}
\end{center}
```

\centering\small
\textit{Pre-89 ATT $= \hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_1 \approx +3.1$ pp; post-88 ATT $= \hat\beta_0 \approx +0.6$ pp.}

```{=latex}
\begin{tikzpicture}[remember picture, overlay]
\node[anchor=south west, inner sep=0pt] at ([xshift=10pt, yshift=10pt]current page.south west) {%
  \navbutton[vintage-forest][fill=BerkeleyBlue, font=\tiny, inner sep=2pt]{ATT by install year $\rightarrow$}%
};
\end{tikzpicture}
```

---

## LUST Discovery: Both Channels Fall

\BerkeleyLightMode

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.15cm}
\begin{center}
\scriptsize
\input{../../Output/Tables/T_LUST_Incumbent_Slide.tex}
\end{center}
```

```{=latex}
\begin{tikzpicture}[remember picture, overlay]
\node[anchor=south west, inner sep=0pt] at ([xshift=10pt, yshift=10pt]current page.south west) {%
  \navbutton[lust-es][fill=BerkeleyBlue, font=\tiny, inner sep=2pt]{LUST event study $\rightarrow$}%
};
\end{tikzpicture}
```

---

## What Closing Firms Do: Composition Shifts to Replacement

\BerkeleyLightMode

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.1cm}
\begin{center}
\scriptsize
\input{../../Output/Tables/T2b_Enriched_Slide.tex}
\end{center}
```

\centering\small
\textit{Conditional on closing (sample is endogenous). Headline: replacement margin nearly doubles ($+53\%$ relative); aggregate exit volume flat.}

---


##

\BerkeleyMode

::: {.layout-statement}
\begin{center}
\Huge \textbf{Structural Model}
\end{center}
:::

---

## Model Setup

\BerkeleyLightMode

**State.** $s_t = (a_t, W_i, R_i, \varepsilon_t)$ — 9 age bins $\times$ 2 wall types $\times$ 2 regimes $= 36$ states. Actions: maintain / exit / replace.

**Flow utility** (annual net revenue $R$ normalized to 1; *not directly observed* — the model identifies $\theta$ in units of $R$).

\begin{align*}
u^m(s) &= 1 - \gamma_p\, p_t - \gamma_r\, h(a_t)\, L \\
u^e(s) &= \kappa \quad \text{(scrap value)} \\
u^r(s) &= -K \quad \text{(install new DW tank)}
\end{align*}

**Calibrated upstream** *(outside the model)*: premium $p_t$, hazard $h(a_t)$, liability $L$ — all from the descriptive-evidence section.

**Estimated jointly** *(inside the model)*: $\theta = (\kappa, K, \gamma_p, \gamma_r)$. NPL match of choice shares across $(a, W, R)$ cells.

---

## Identification

\BerkeleyLightMode

Each parameter is identified by a distinct moment in the choice data:

- $\kappa$: the unconditional exit rate $P(\text{exit} \mid s)$
- $K$: the replace-vs-exit share among closing tanks
- $\gamma_p$: cross-cell response of choice shares to premium variation in $(a, W, R)$
- $\gamma_r$: divergence in age-closure hazard between uniform-premium and risk-based regimes

NPL (Aguirregabiria \& Mira 2007); AM-2002 profile-likelihood SEs.

---

## Structural Estimating Sample

\BerkeleyLightMode

**Unit of observation.** Facility-year. One row per facility per year, 1999--2018.

**Sample.** Texas facilities, post-reform period. The structural model fits the *equilibrium* of the risk-based regime --- TX is the regime that's actually priced.

- Roughly 26k TX incumbent facilities over 20 years $\approx$ 520k facility-years
- After dropping rows with missing premium or hazard inputs: $\approx$ 430k facility-years
- Choices observed: maintain / exit / replace; mapped onto 32 state cells $(a, W, R)$
- Premiums $p_t$ from Mid-Continent rate-filing reconstruction; hazards $h(a_t)$ from the first-release-prediction model

---

## Parameter Estimates

\BerkeleyLightMode

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.3cm}
\begin{center}
\input{../../Output/Tables/04f_Theta_Table_AM_SE.tex}
\end{center}
```

---

## Estimates in Economic Terms

\BerkeleyLightMode

\textit{Reminder: all parameters are in units of annual net revenue $R$. Dollar translations below use a calibration anchor of $R \approx \$10$k/yr per facility }

- **$\hat\kappa \approx 26\,R$** (about \$261k under the calibration). Net scrap value when a facility exits; large in absolute terms, consistent with land-value liquidation net of remediation liability.

- **$\hat K \approx 5\,R$** (about \$52k). Replacement cost for a new double-walled tank; in line with industry estimates.

- **$\hat\gamma_p = -0.29$.**  a one-unit ($\approx 1\,R$) increase in premium reduces the maintain-flow value by 0.29 units.

- **$\hat\gamma_r = 0.16$.**  The point estimate is small relative to a fully-internalized benchmark ($\gamma_r = 1$).

---

## Counterfactual Policy Scenarios

\BerkeleyLightMode

| Policy | Mechanism |
|:--|:--|
| **Subsidy** | $K \to 0.5\, K$ — replacement cost halved |
| **Pigouvian** | Add $h(a)\cdot E$ to maintain cost; $E = \$17{,}000$ (Marcus, 2021) |
| **Mandate** | Force $P(\text{maintain}) = 0$ for $a \geq 6$ (age 25+) |

**What we do for each policy.** Recompute firms' optimal choices under the new price/cost regime, let the fleet settle into its long-run age-and-wall distribution, then add up the present-value impact on three groups: firms (operating profits), the environment (avoided cleanup and health damages), and the government (subsidies paid out).

---

## Welfare Decomposition

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/04j_4p_Welfare_BarChart.pdf}
\end{center}

---

## Counterfactual Welfare Summary

\BerkeleyLightMode

```{=latex}
\vspace{0.4cm}
\begin{center}
\input{../../Output/Tables/04j_Welfare_4p_Summary.tex}
\end{center}
```

\vfill

\centering
\textit{All three CFs reduce social welfare at $E = \$17$k. Subsidy hurts most: govt outlay outweighs firm-surplus gain. Mandate roughly neutral.}

---

## Takeaways

\BerkeleyLightMode

1. **Risk-based pricing does shift behavior.** A $+1.58$ pp annual closure ATT — about $4\times$ larger in the pre-1989 SW-heavy stock that the schedule actually prices.

2. **The shift is on the composition margin, not exit volume.** Conditional on closing, the **replacement** rate nearly doubles ($12\% \to 18\%$); net tank counts and capacity barely move. LUST discovery falls through both background and inspection-triggered channels.

3. **Welfare under the priced regime is still below first-best.** All three counterfactual policies — $K$ subsidy, Pigouvian surcharge on $h(a)\cdot E$, age-25 mandate — *reduce* social welfare at $E = \$17$k. RBP is a partial fix; closing the residual wedge requires direct intervention beyond the actuarial price signal.

---

## Appendix: ATT by Installation Vintage {visibility="uncounted"}

\BerkeleyLightMode

```{=latex}
\hypertarget{vintage-forest}{}
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Fig_Vintage_Forest.pdf}
\end{center}

---

## Appendix: Cell-Level Goodness of Fit {visibility="uncounted"}

\BerkeleyLightMode

```{=latex}
\hypertarget{cell-gof}{}
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Figure_CV_CellRisk_GoF.png}
\end{center}

---

## Appendix: Premium vs. Per-Tank Expected Loss {visibility="uncounted"}

\BerkeleyLightMode

```{=latex}
\hypertarget{premium-vs-el}{}
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Figure_Premium_vs_EL_Raw.png}
\end{center}

---

## Appendix: LUST Event Study --- Total Leak Discovery {visibility="uncounted"}

\BerkeleyLightMode

```{=latex}
\hypertarget{lust-es}{}
```

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/Figure_ES_LUSTTotal_Appendix.pdf}
\end{center}

---

## Appendix: Subsidy Curve {visibility="uncounted"}

\BerkeleyLightMode

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.78\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../../Output/Figures/04j_4p_Subsidy_Curve.pdf}
\end{center}
